title:
Patient Report
Redesign
subtitle: From a blank page into a structured, shareable communication tool.

slides.title:
Patient Report
Redesign
slides.description: Doctors were skipping the patient report — not because they didn't care, but because the tool gave them a blank page and no place to start. I redesigned it so the hard part is done before they open it.

slides.label: The Problem
slides.title: A blank page after every appointment
slides.content: No structure, no templates, no annotation tools.
issues: Sidebar locked doctor info into 25% of the screen
No templates — every report started blank
Raw scan images with no annotation tools
PDF output not readable by patients
slides.highlight: The tool was designed around the software — not the doctor using it.

image.caption: Old — three-panel split, static sidebar, blank content area

slides.label: User Research
slides.title: What doctors told me
slides.content: I interviewed doctors across four specialties. The same problems came up every time.

quotes.text: I just skip the report most of the time. It takes too long.
quotes.author: Dr. A, General Practice

quotes.text: Patients ask me to explain things the report doesn't show.
quotes.author: Dr. B, Restorative

quotes.text: I have no way to circle what I'm talking about on the image.
quotes.author: Dr. C, Orthodontics

quotes.text: Every report looks different — there's no standard starting point.
quotes.author: Dr. D, Implantology

slides.highlight: These problems had gone unreported — doctors assumed it was just how the tool worked.

slides.label: Design Goals
slides.title: What I set out to fix

goals.title: Give the canvas back
goals.description: Move metadata and actions to the header — full screen for editing.

goals.title: Give doctors a starting point
goals.description: Procedure templates so reports start structured, not blank.

goals.title: Make findings visual
goals.description: Annotation and tooth tagging — show, don't describe.

kpis: Time to create a complete report

slides.label: Ideation
slides.title: Layout directions I explored
slides.dir1Desc: Collapsible sidebar — kept the three-panel layout but let doctors hide the right panel. Felt incremental. Doctors were still managing the interface instead of just writing the report.
slides.dir2Desc: Floating action drawer — tools and metadata in an overlay. Added interaction without solving the space problem.
slides.dir3Desc: Compact header bar — moved all metadata and actions into a single top strip, freeing the full canvas for the report. Solved the space problem without adding new interaction patterns.

slides.label: UI Layout
slides.title: From three fixed panels to a full editing canvas
slides.beforeDescription: A fixed right panel locked doctor info, preview, and sharing — the editing area squeezed in the middle.
slides.afterDescription: Everything moved to the header — doctor, clinic, patient, actions inline.
beforeBullets: Right panel permanently occupied with static info
Content squeezed between two fixed columns
Share and Export buried outside the editing flow
afterBullets: Doctor, clinic, and patient editable from the header
Share and Export PDF always within reach

slides.label: Templates & Sections
slides.title: A structured starting point — built to flex
slides.content: I built five procedure templates — so doctors start with structure, not a blank page.
issues: Five templates — General Scan, Implant, Crown Prep, Follow-up, Custom
Each pre-loaded with sections for that procedure type
Modular blocks: Image, Before/After, Clinical Details, Cost, Notes
Drag to reorder, one-tap duplicate or delete
slides.highlight: Templates give doctors a running start. Blocks keep them in control.

slides.label: Annotation & Tooth Chart
slides.title: From plain scan images to annotated findings
slides.content: I added two tools for one question: where exactly is the problem?
issues: Pen, text, and color tools — annotate at full size
Tooth chart — click to tag affected teeth, shown as numbered markers
Findings tied to anatomy — not described in a text block
Patients see which teeth are affected
slides.highlight: Show, don't describe — the principle behind both tools.

slides.label: Share & Export
slides.title: From three basic buttons to a full sharing system
slides.beforeDescription: Three sidebar buttons — Preview, Download PDF, Share via QR.
slides.afterDescription: I replaced them with a full sharing modal — email invites, access permissions, PIN protection, multi-channel delivery.
beforeBullets: Three isolated actions with no shared logic
QR code was the only real sharing option
No way to invite specific recipients
afterBullets: Invite by email with ownership and access levels
Multi-channel delivery — WhatsApp, WeChat, QR, copy link

slides.label: Doctor Feedback
slides.quote: The templates actually match what I do, and everything I need is right there in front of me.
slides.author: Dr. N. Haddad
slides.context: Feedback collected during the iTero early access program, before full rollout.

outcomes.title: Report creation
outcomes.description: The template system removed the blank-page barrier. Doctors in the early access group started and completed reports they would have skipped.

outcomes.title: Clinical communication
outcomes.description: Annotation and tooth tagging let doctors mark the exact finding instead of describing it in text — clearer for patients, faster for the doctor.

outcomes.title: Completion rate
outcomes.description: With structure upfront, doctors who previously skipped reporting started completing it. Broader measurement is planned post-rollout.

outcomes.title: Reach per report
outcomes.description: Multi-channel sharing — email, WhatsApp, WeChat, QR code — meant reports reached patients on the channel they actually check.

slides.label: Reflection
slides.title: What I'd do differently
whatWorked: Defining KPIs before design work kept every decision tied to an outcome.
The modular block system — customizable structure was the right balance.
whatFailed: I scoped annotation too late — it triggered a full rework after the first doctor feedback round.
whatYoudDoDifferently: Test the layout with actual doctors before locking it — internal reviews weren't enough.
Define sharing permissions with engineering earlier — the edge cases compressed the handoff.
slides.whatYouLearned: Friction isn't bad UI — it's software that makes professionals manage it. Fix the default.
slides.whatYouCouldntMeasure: Long-term adoption and whether templates reduced variability — needs 6+ months of data.

slides.title: Thank You
slides.subtitle: I make complex workflows feel obvious — a skill for any high-pressure product.
